question: When designing a metrics system for an organization, what distinguishes a 'leading indicator' from a 'lagging indicator,' and why does the distinction matter?
options: Leading and lagging indicators measure the same things at different points in the year

Leading indicators predict future outcomes (they change before the thing they predict changes); lagging indicators measure outcomes that have already occurred: Lagging indicators (retrospective): revenue, profit, customer churn, employee turnover — these tell you what happened; by the time they signal a problem, the cause is already weeks or months old; example: quarterly revenue decline reveals that the business lost customers in the previous quarter. Leading indicators (predictive): sales pipeline size, NPS score, employee engagement score, R&D spending, customer onboarding completion rate — these predict future outcomes; example: a declining NPS score today predicts higher customer churn in 3-6 months (before it shows up in revenue); the management value: leading indicators allow intervention before the damage appears in lagging metrics; a manager who sees NPS declining can investigate and fix the root cause before customers churn; a manager who only sees revenue after customers have churned has limited ability to prevent the loss; the design challenge: leading indicators are harder to identify (requires understanding causal mechanisms) and often harder to measure; companies tend to over-rely on lagging financial metrics because they are easy to measure and report
All metrics measure outcomes and the 'leading' distinction is a theoretical concept only
Only financial metrics can serve as leading indicators of business performance
explanation: The leading indicator identification challenge: the most useful leading indicators are specific to each business's causal mechanisms. For an SaaS company: feature adoption rate in week 1 (predicts 90-day retention) is a leading indicator for long-term revenue. For a retailer: foot traffic counts (leading indicator for same-store sales). For a bank: early-stage delinquency rate (30-60 days past due, predicting eventual charge-offs). The causal chain discipline: identify what you ultimately care about (revenue, profit) → work backward through the causal chain: what behaviors or metrics predict the outcomes? These are your leading indicators. The Amazon feedback loop: Amazon tracks 'defect rate' (percentage of orders with any problem) as a leading indicator for customer satisfaction and retention. A rising defect rate predicts declining NPS and eventually declining revenue if uncorrected. The danger of optimizing only lagging indicators: teams that are measured only on quarterly revenue will take actions (pushing sales through discounts, delaying expenses) that improve the lagging metric while harming the leading indicators (customer satisfaction, product quality) — destroying future performance while appearing to improve current performance.

question: 'Vanity metrics' are measurements that look impressive but don't reflect underlying business health. Which of the following is most likely a vanity metric for a startup?
options: Monthly Active Users is never a vanity metric for any business

Total app downloads (downloads don't indicate engagement, retention, or revenue); the reason 'downloads' is a vanity metric for most apps: (1) Downloads measure marketing reach, not product-market fit; an app can be downloaded 1M times by users who open it once and never return; (2) Downloads don't predict revenue (a free app that nobody engages with generates no revenue regardless of download count); (3) Downloads can be inflated through paid install campaigns with no quality filter (you can buy 100,000 downloads from low-quality traffic for $50,000; these downloads have near-zero engagement); contrast with real engagement metrics: DAU/MAU ratio (Daily Active Users / Monthly Active Users = 'stickiness'; Snapchat's DAU/MAU is ~65%, meaning users come back daily; Facebook is ~65%; apps below 20% are generally considered poorly retained); Retention cohort (what % of users who downloaded in Week 1 are still using the app in Week 4, Week 12, Week 52?); Revenue per user or ARPU. The 'growth metric' trap in startups: investors and founders sometimes optimize for metrics that look good in pitch decks without connection to business value; downloads, registered users, social media followers, and press mentions are classic vanity metrics when they're the primary focus
Registered users is the best metric for evaluating a social media platform's health
Gross merchandise volume always indicates a healthy e-commerce marketplace
explanation: Vanity metrics vs. actionable metrics: Eric Ries (Lean Startup) popularized the 'vanity metric' concept — measurements that make teams feel good but don't guide decisions. The test for a vanity metric: 'If this number changed, what action would I take?' If the answer is 'nothing' or 'unclear,' it may be a vanity metric. Total downloads: if downloads double, should you double the marketing budget? Not necessarily — if the retention rate is 2%, you're growing a leak. If retained users (30-day retention) double, that's actionable — the product is working. The Twitter/X follower count parallel: brands obsess over follower counts on social media; follower counts don't directly generate revenue; engagement rate (likes, comments, shares per follower) and revenue generated from social posts are more actionable. The fundraising vanity metric danger: startups that optimize for fundraising-facing metrics (total users, app downloads, revenue run rate reported in the most favorable way) can build organizations that look impressive in investor meetings but have fundamentally broken unit economics.

question: Uber's management in 2014-2016 tracked 'Gross Bookings' (total value of all rides, before Uber pays drivers) as its primary metric. Critics said this massively overstated Uber's business because Uber's actual revenue (net of driver payments) was only 20-25% of gross bookings. Which perspective is right, and what does this metric choice reveal about strategic priorities?
options: Gross Bookings is always a more accurate representation of business performance than Net Revenue

Both metrics are accurate; they reveal different things — the metric choice reflects strategic priorities: Gross Bookings argument: (1) Gross Bookings captures the total economic value Uber facilitates in the transportation market; (2) For a two-sided marketplace (connecting riders and drivers), the marketplace's scale is better captured by total transaction value; Amazon reports GMV (Gross Merchandise Volume) for its marketplace for the same reason; (3) Gross Bookings growth shows market penetration and platform scale; (4) As Uber's take rate (the % it keeps) changes (e.g., through pricing strategy), revenue would change even if booking volume didn't — gross bookings is stable. Net Revenue argument: (1) Net Revenue = the money Uber actually earns and must cover its operating costs with; (2) Gross Bookings growth without Net Revenue growth means Uber is growing the business but not capturing value (lower take rates); (3) For financial sustainability analysis, net revenue and margins matter; Uber can't pay salaries from Gross Bookings; (4) If Uber's strategic priority is scale (becoming the dominant platform that competitors can't displace), then Gross Bookings is the right primary metric; if the strategic priority is profitability, Net Revenue and unit economics are the right metrics; (5) The danger: Uber used high Gross Bookings numbers in pitches while losing $2-3B/year in EBITDA; investors who didn't look past Gross Bookings to unit economics may have overestimated Uber's financial health
Uber should have used Revenue Per Driver as its primary metric
All marketplace businesses should use identical metrics to enable direct comparison
explanation: The platform metric definition debate: Uber, Airbnb, and other marketplace companies have debated which revenue recognition principle to use (principal vs. agent accounting). If Uber is a 'principal' (primarily responsible for the transportation service), it would report full booking value as revenue. If Uber is an 'agent' (just connecting riders and drivers), it reports only its take rate as revenue. The SEC's guidance: Uber moved from reporting gross bookings as primary metric to reporting 'Mobility Revenue' (net of driver payments) and separately reporting 'Gross Bookings' as a supplemental metric, after SEC guidance. The investor confusion period: Uber's $82B IPO valuation (2019) was based partly on Gross Bookings multiples; after the IPO, as analysts focused more on Net Revenue and unit economics, the stock fell 35% in its first year. The DoorDash parallel: DoorDash reports 'Marketplace GOV' (Gross Order Value) as its primary scale metric while reporting Revenue (net of delivery fees, service fees, restaurant commission) as the financial metric — recognizing that both have strategic relevance.

question: A retail bank tracks customer satisfaction (NPS = 42), number of products per customer (2.3), and deposit balance growth (8%/year). An analyst suggests: 'We should also track which customers are most profitable and what behaviors predict customer lifetime value.' The CFO says: 'All customers are valued.' Who has the better argument?
options: All bank customers generate equivalent value and should receive identical service strategies

The analyst is correct — customer profitability and LTV analysis enables strategic resource allocation without abandoning service quality: the Pareto reality in banking: the top 20% of customers typically generate 80-90% of bank profit; the bottom 20% may be unprofitable (low balances, high service costs, low product penetration); the CFO's 'all customers are valued' may mean 'we serve all customers well' (appropriate service standard) but shouldn't mean 'we deploy identical resources to all customers'; the customer profitability framework: Revenue per customer: net interest income (loan spread), fee income (overdraft, wire transfer, maintenance fees), cross-sell product revenue; Cost to serve: transaction volume (high volume = higher ops cost), branch usage vs. digital channel (branch is 10× more expensive per interaction than digital), support contacts, risk cost (defaults, fraud); Profitable customers: high balances (generate NIM), multiple products (cross-sell revenue, switching cost), digital channel preference (low cost), low credit risk; Unprofitable customers: low balances, single product, high branch usage, high support contacts; the strategic implication: resources (relationship manager time, product development, marketing) should be concentrated where they create the most value; unprofitable customers aren't necessarily mistreated — they're encouraged toward self-service (lower cost); the ethical boundary: 'de-marketing' to unprofitable customers (making their service experience deliberately worse) crosses an ethical line; 'efficiently serving' them (directing them to lower-cost channels) is legitimate
Banks should charge unprofitable customers premium prices to improve their profitability
Customer profitability analysis is only relevant for investment banks, not retail banks
explanation: Customer profitability analysis (CPA) in banking: the discipline of calculating per-customer profitability is a standard banking practice. The key components: Funds Transfer Pricing (FTP) allocates NIM (net interest margin) to the deposit or loan generating it; Activity-Based Costing (ABC) allocates operational costs to the customers who use each service; risk cost is allocated based on credit risk profile. The Bank of America example: BofA segments customers by 'Preferred Rewards' status (based on combined deposit and investment balances) — customers with $20K+ in combined balances receive fee waivers, rate improvements, and enhanced service (more relationship manager access). This isn't discriminatory — it reflects that high-balance customers generate more value and receive more value in return. The CFO's concern: there is a legitimate ethical concern with customer profitability analysis — it can lead to 'redlining' (denying services to low-income customers who tend to be in protected demographic groups). Federal banking regulations prohibit using profitability analysis in ways that create disparate impact on protected classes. The appropriate ethical standard: using CPA to allocate marketing investment (promote the premium savings account to high-balance customers) is legitimate; using CPA to deny basic banking services is not.

question: Facebook (now Meta) tracked Daily Active Users (DAU) and Monthly Active Users (MAU) for years as its primary metrics. Critics argued that DAU/MAU overstated engagement by counting users who didn't intentionally visit (because Facebook pixels and trackers counted page views on third-party sites as 'active'). What does this case reveal about the relationship between metric design and business transparency?
options: Tracking DAU and MAU is always a valid and unambiguous way to measure social media engagement

Metric definitions have strategic implications; how a metric is defined and what it includes determines what it reveals and conceals: the DAU definition controversy: Meta counted 'daily active user' broadly — any Facebook interaction counted, including: intentional app opens, news feed scrolls, clicking a Like button; loading any webpage with a Facebook pixel (which is essentially any major website) if the user was logged into Facebook; this broad definition inflated DAU counts relative to 'intentional engagement'; the strategic implication: Meta's ad revenue depends on showing ads to genuinely engaged users; if DAU counts include passive impressions from users who didn't intentionally open Facebook, the advertisers paying for 'engagement' may be overpaying for lower-quality impressions; the metric selection bias: companies naturally select metric definitions that make performance look best; this is rational individually but creates collective misrepresentation; external audit value: the SEC's enforcement of advertising disclosure requirements requires companies to define their metrics clearly in financial filings; Meta's 10-K disclosures include extensive footnotes about what 'active user' means; the broader principle: when a metric is used both internally (to manage the business) and externally (to represent value to investors and partners), there is an incentive to define the metric in the most favorable way possible; this creates an information asymmetry that third parties (investors, regulators, advertisers) must manage through standardized definitions, third-party audits, or skeptical analysis
Social media metrics are inherently unmeasurable and all DAU/MAU figures should be ignored
Meta's metric definitions were definitively fraudulent and resulted in legal consequences
explanation: Metric gaming in platform businesses: the history of platform metrics is filled with examples where the metric definition changed to show more favorable performance. Twitter's active user controversy: Twitter reported 'monetizable daily active users' (mDAU) rather than DAU — excluding users who saw no ads; this was partially honest (only counting users the business could monetize) but also convenient (excluding bots and low-engagement users made the number look better on a per-user basis). Snap's DAU: Snap counts users who open the Snapchat app (an intentional action) — a stricter definition than Meta's. The regulatory response: the FTC and SEC have both scrutinized how social media and tech companies define and report user metrics. The CNET lawsuit: in 2021, a lawsuit alleged Meta knew its video metrics were inflated (counting views of a few seconds as equivalent to longer views) — affecting decisions by publishers who devoted resources to video based on inflated metrics. The audit approach: the Media Rating Council (MRC) audits digital advertising measurement methodologies; MRC accreditation is the industry's attempt to create standardized, third-party verified metrics that reduce metric gaming.

question: A hospital system creates a strategy for reducing patient readmission rates — when patients return to the hospital within 30 days of discharge. They set a hospital-level metric: 'Reduce 30-day readmission rate from 15% to 12%.' Six months later, the metric shows 11.5% readmission — better than target. A quality audit reveals that some discharge nurses are delaying discharges when patients are borderline stable, to 'protect' the metric. How does this case illustrate the risk of metric gaming?
options: The hospital's metric was poorly designed because readmission rates are impossible to reduce

Goodhart's Law in action: once a metric becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure; optimizing the metric can harm the underlying goal: the intended outcome: reduce preventable readmissions (patients who are discharged before they're fully stable); the metric: 30-day readmission rate; the gaming behavior: delaying discharge of borderline patients (keeping them in hospital 1-2 extra days to reduce the probability they return within 30 days); the consequences: (1) Delayed discharge harms patients: patients who stay in hospital unnecessarily are exposed to hospital-acquired infections, reduced mobility, and disrupted normal routine; (2) The metric improves, but the goal (better discharge decision-making) is harmed; (3) Hospital capacity: delayed discharges reduce bed availability for patients who need to be admitted; (4) Cost: unnecessary hospital days cost $3,000-5,000/day; the metric-goal misalignment: the metric (readmission rate) was a proxy for the goal (good discharge decisions and care transitions); optimizing the proxy without regard to the goal produces behavior that helps the proxy while harming the goal. The diagnostic fix: add metrics that catch the gaming: average length of stay by physician (if a physician's LOS rises at the same time readmission improves, the gaming is visible); discharge disposition quality score; time from discharge decision to actual discharge
Metric gaming by clinical staff never occurs in healthcare settings
The hospital should eliminate all readmission metrics to prevent gaming behavior
explanation: Goodhart's Law in healthcare: readmission rate gaming is a documented phenomenon in US hospitals. When CMS (Medicare) introduced readmission penalties (the Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program, 2012), hospitals faced financial penalties for excessive readmissions. Research documented that some hospitals responded by: (1) Holding patients in 'observation status' (not officially admitting them) — these patients don't count toward readmission metrics if they return; (2) More selective discharge (keeping borderline patients longer); and (3) Improved care transitions (the intended behavior). The multi-metric solution: Goodhart's Law suggests that no single metric is safe from gaming; the solution is a 'dashboard' of metrics that are hard to simultaneously game: readmission rate + length of stay + patient satisfaction + cost per episode. Gaming one metric will often negatively affect another, creating a self-correcting system. The unintended consequences principle: any metric used in high-stakes decisions (where gaming has significant reward) will be gamed by rational actors. Metric designers must anticipate gaming strategies and either build in controls or use multiple metrics that constrain gaming behavior.

question: A startup's board of directors has three proposed frameworks for the company's strategic metrics: (A) One primary metric ('North Star Metric') that the whole company optimizes for; (B) A Balanced Scorecard with 15-20 metrics across four perspectives; (C) An 'OKR system' with 3-5 company-level objectives, each with 3-5 key results. What are the trade-offs of each approach and which is most appropriate for a growth-stage startup with 150 employees?
options: All three frameworks are equivalent for all organizations at all stages

Each framework has different alignment, flexibility, and communication trade-offs that match different organizational contexts: (A) North Star Metric (NSM): best for alignment — everyone in the company knows the one metric they're trying to move; examples: Airbnb = nights booked; Facebook = daily active users; Spotify = time spent listening; trade-off: oversimplification (one metric can be gamed, may optimize for one dimension while neglecting others; a company that optimizes NSM perfectly may improve the metric while destroying profitability or customer experience quality); appropriate when: the business is simple enough that one metric captures the whole strategy. (B) Balanced Scorecard (15-20 metrics): appropriate for large, complex organizations with stable strategy; too complex for a 150-person startup — creating and maintaining 20 metrics requires significant process overhead; nobody can hold 20 metrics in their head simultaneously; appropriate when: strategy is stable (BSC is designed for multi-year strategy execution, not quarterly pivots), the organization is large enough to dedicate resources to metric collection and reporting, and cross-functional alignment at the senior leadership level is the primary coordination challenge. (C) OKR system (3-5 objectives × 3-5 KRs): best match for a 150-person growth-stage startup: objectives provide directional clarity ('become the leading project management tool for distributed teams'), key results provide measurable specificity ('increase DAU by 40%', 'reduce time-to-value for new users from 2 days to 4 hours'); quarterly cadence allows adaptation without complete strategic overhaul; appropriate for: growth-stage companies where direction changes frequently enough that a rigid framework is too slow, but coordination across 150 people requires more than informal alignment
Startups should never use formal metric frameworks until they reach 500 employees
North Star Metrics are always superior to balanced scorecard approaches
explanation: OKR adoption in growth-stage companies: John Doerr introduced OKRs to Google in 1999 (when Google had ~40 employees); Google used OKRs from employee #40 to 100,000+, adapting the cadence and level of detail as the company scaled. The OKR framework's flexibility: quarterly OKRs enable course correction without abandoning the framework; annual company-level OKRs provide strategic continuity. The North Star Metric's simplicity advantage: Airbnb's 'nights booked' is reportedly understood by every employee — from engineer to cleaning coordinator — and creates a shared sense of direction that complex frameworks can't replicate. The risk: Airbnb's focus on nights booked didn't directly protect host quality or safety (which affected nights booked indirectly but through a delayed causal chain). The 15-metric BSC problem for startups: collecting 15 metrics quarterly requires infrastructure (data pipelines, reporting tools, analyst time) that a 150-person startup doesn't have. The quarterly review meeting that covers 15 metrics is 3-4 hours of leadership time — probably too slow for startup decision-making. Intel's OKR origin: Andy Grove created OKRs at Intel to coordinate the massive, complex execution of semiconductor manufacturing — a context where one number couldn't capture the strategy's breadth. Doerr adapted Grove's framework for Google.
